# Hedge-audit shard: tasks — consolidated report (2026-07-11)

Procedure: `analysis/2026-07-11-gap-fork-provenance-audit-brief-template.md`, applied per item.
Block `tasks` (array key `tasks`). All 9 items read fresh at audit time; **all 9 are status `planned` (unbuilt)** — a material fact: for an unbuilt task, modal/fork language about implementation mechanism is presumptively genuine remaining design ambiguity, not invented augmentation, unless a cited source actually determined one answer and the filing forked it anyway.

Binding convention checked once for the whole shard: `pi-mono-is-exemplar` (declarative-population / locally-editable-installed / two-tier-config / named-default-constants). It is silent on every flagged clause below — none of the flagged forks is a population/config/default question — so it is not a tiebreaker for any item and is not cited as one.

**Shard verdict: 0 corrections.** Every flagged clause is one of: (a) verbatim/near-verbatim from the source the item cites, (b) a naming placeholder for an as-yet-unnamed op (not a resolution fork), or (c) a source-grounded deferral of an unbuilt design point. No item carries an LLM-augmentation-with-no-basis fork.

2414:      "description": "User directive (verbatim, this session): 'first we need a script that will use nlp processing to pre-identify candidates needing full agent treatment for validating / de-hedging / correcting, for all substrate items, not just gaps.' Implemented as scripts/scan-substrate-hedges.ts (read-only, informational, non-gate; matching this repo's scan-comment-citations.ts precedent): scans EVERY block kind declared in the active substrate's config (config.block_kinds via loadConfig -- no hardcoded block list, per this project's derive-from-registry convention) and every schema-declared prose field of every item (a string property counts as prose iff its schema declares no enum/pattern/format/const -- ids, statuses, dates, hashes all declare one; the walk recurses through arrays-of-strings and arrays-of-objects; x-prompt-budget presence is recorded on findings but not required -- no hardcoded field list). All substrate access flows through the canonical pi-context surface (loadConfig / readSchema / readBlock), never raw fs reads of the substrate's *.json. Heuristic pattern matching in three weighted categories, grounded in the hedges the manual FGAP-124/125/126/127 provenance audits actually confirmed: (1) fork -- enumerated-alternative language (and/or; either...or; dash-or as in FGAP-125's live '-- or amend the schema'; semicolon-or; paren-or and or+gerund as in pre-correction FGAP-126; lettered '(a)...(b)' option lists -- numbered '(1)...(2)' deliberately unmatched because live substrate prose uses it to enumerate facts, not alternatives; 'alternatively'; option-enumeration cues); (2) deferral -- explicit decision-postponement ('as a decision' -- the exact FGAP-126 augmentation phrase -- TBD, to be determined/decided, not yet decided, open question, user's / user scope call, up to the user, undecided/undetermined, deferred, left/remains open, pending/needs a decision, decide later, punt, weak 'not yet'); (3) modal-hedge -- low-weight uncertainty vocabulary (might, could, may need/want/require, possibly/perhaps, probably/presumably, eventually, at some point, unclear/uncertain, unknown, later, seems/appears to, if needed). Every match carries pattern id + category + weight (1-4); an item's score is the summed weight across its fields, candidates rank by score with a min-score threshold (default 2, --min-score). Output: a single machine-readable JSON report (tool / generated_at / minScore / per-block summaries / reconciled totals / candidates sorted by score; per match: field path, matched text, surrounding-context snippet) written to a timestamped path under gitignored tmp/substrate-hedge-scans/ by default; --output overrides the path, --stdout prints the full report instead. The ranked candidate list is what an orchestrator works through with the gap-fork-provenance-audit-brief-template (analysis/2026-07-11-gap-fork-provenance-audit-brief-template.md) or its generalization to other block kinds. Weights rank, they do not judge: grounded forks (FGAP-124's and FGAP-127's were audited and left standing) are still flagged by design -- grounded-vs-invented is exactly what the downstream per-item agent audit determines; recall is deliberately favored over precision. The script itself corrects nothing and makes zero substrate writes -- pre-identification only.",

# Schema-versioning design-intent archaeology

Answers the user's verbatim question (2026-07-11 ~12:34 UTC / 20:34 Asia/Shanghai, never answered before the conversation moved to an urgent fix): "what does claude-history have to say about schema versions and substrate items? I suspect we've have partially implemented / unfinished elements thrown around with no clear overarching understanding of intended end state."

Scope: read-only claude-history archaeology of pi-context's schema-versioning + migration mechanism (schema `version`, block `schema_version`, config's own `schema_version`, migrations.json's own envelope version, per-schema `fromVersion`/`toVersion` migration declarations, `installed_schemas`/`installed_blocks`/`installed_from` install-baseline). No code was re-derived; the six-concept inventory is taken as given from the prior same-session code investigation.

## Verdict

**Corroborated, with one qualification.** The mechanism accreted piecemeal across roughly ten weeks (2026-05-09 → 2026-07-11). At no point in claude-history does a single planning conversation lay out all six version-shaped concepts and how they reconcile before any of them were built. Each session solved the next immediate problem in front of it. The qualification: the *earliest* slice (schema `$id`/`version` + the migration-registry primitives) was NOT ad hoc — it was step 4 of an explicit, pre-written, multi-step plan (the "pi-context rebuild arc"). But that plan's own stated scope was narrow (closing FGAP-006 + surfacing FGAP-016) and never claimed to be the full versioning contract. Everything built on top of it — config's own migrate-on-load, the block-wide `schema_version` envelope, migrations.json's own envelope version, and the `installed_schemas`/`installed_blocks`/install-baseline reconciliation — arrived later as separate, independently-discovered gaps that were only retroactively bound together into one feature once enough of them had piled up to look like a pattern.

## Direct answers to the five investigation questions

1. **Was there ever one planning conversation laying out the full six-concept contract before any of it was built?** No. The only pre-written plan found (`idempotent-dancing-wilkes.md`, step 4 of the "pi-context rebuild arc," 2026-05-09) scoped itself explicitly to schema `$id`/`version` + the migration-registry primitives + `validateBlockWithMigration`. It says nothing about config's own `schema_version`, migrations.json's own envelope, or `installed_schemas`/install-baseline — those concepts did not exist at plan-writing time.

2. **Was each subsequent piece motivated by returning to that original design, or by a freshly-discovered gap?** Freshly-discovered gaps in every case found: FGAP-050 (2026-06-06), FGAP-095/096/099 (2026-06-18), FGAP-065's block-materialization angle (refiled 2026-06-07/06-20), FGAP-140/141 (2026-07-10/11). None of the filing text found references the 2026-05-09 plan as a design to complete; each stands on its own observed symptom.

3. **Did the user ever push back on fragmentation of this specific mechanism, the way they've pushed back on invented forks/scope creep elsewhere?** No instance found in claude-history. Searches for pushback phrasing turned up nothing tied to schema-versioning/migrations specifically. The user's session-current question (the one this report answers) is itself the first surfaced pushback on the fragmentation — not something raised earlier and then re-litigated.

4. **Did any assistant, in any earlier session, flag "this needs a unified design" for this mechanism before now?** No instance found. The closest thing is procedural, not architectural: commit/plan language repeatedly marks pieces as deliberately partial ("foundational closure," "consumer migration, future step," "deferred to step 5+") — i.e., incompleteness was named locally, turn by turn, but no session stepped back to say the six concepts as a whole lacked a reconciling spec. That observation is new to the code-half investigation this session, not something claude-history shows being raised earlier.

## Bottom line for the user's original question

The extension code's version-shaped concepts are real and load-bearing (schema `version`, block `schema_version`, config's `schema_version`, migrations.json's own envelope, per-schema `fromVersion`/`toVersion`, and the install-baseline `installed_schemas`/`installed_blocks`/`installed_from`). claude-history shows they were built in that order, over ten weeks, by five-plus independent sessions each closing the gap in front of it — not from one design that was executed piece by piece. The one exception is the innermost primitive (schema `version` + migration registry itself), which WAS pre-planned as step 4 of a named multi-step arc; everything reconciling it with config/migrations.json/install-time behavior came later, unplanned, gap-by-gap. Your suspicion is correct for the mechanism as a whole; it is only partly correct for its oldest component.

runs.output: 318 words

**Autonomous-loop authorization (the `mandate-008` exception for the deterministic loop, binding).** `mandate-008` — a subagent returns an issue → STOP, report, the user decides — governs when the continue/stop signal is an LLM judgement (a blocker, an ambiguity, a scope/value/authority call). It does NOT stop the iterate-to-zero loop of steps 2→3→4. Autonomous re-dispatch of a FRESH agent (a fix agent, then a fresh adversarial re-audit) IS permitted and REQUIRED iff the loop terminates on a DETERMINISTIC gate — `npm run build` + `npm run check` + `npm test` exit 0 and the runtime-demo real-check, never an LLM read of "looks clean" — AND a SEPARATE fresh adversarial audit (not the implementer) reports every acceptance criterion CONFIRMED. `done ≡ (deterministic gate exit 0) AND (every success criterion CONFIRMED)` — even if the audit's total findings are nonzero. **Audit posture:** the audit brief carries the task's filed `acceptance_criteria` verbatim; the auditor reports each criterion by id as CONFIRMED or a finding, never grades severity, never recommends; the orchestrator computes GO/NO-GO per criterion (any criterion false → NO-GO), and the per-criterion verdicts are filed as the verification item the turn the audit returns. Inside the loop the orchestrator ends each turn by dispatching the next agent (fix → re-audit → fix …) — its message terminates in the next tool call, never in a report that yields control — and surfaces to the user ONLY at a genuine LLM-judgement fork (a scope/value/authority decision, e.g. a HELD merge or release per the release-hold). A criterion-falsifying finding from the audit/probe is work the loop closes autonomously, scoped to the finding's whole class, not an issue handed to the user; handing a policy-determined criterion-falsifying fix to the user instead of looping it is the mandate-008 misread this exception forecloses. A finding that falsifies no criterion is FILED and not fixed in this task; trivia (cosmetic/style, language WARNs, pre-existing/unrelated/flaky) is non-blocking and recorded as a Flag.
